fieldGuide.name: A visit can't be delivered
fieldGuide.remember: >30 min late = "late". >60 min or non-delivery = "missed". Both must be reported.
fieldGuide.whatItIs: Any time a scheduled visit doesn't happen for ANY reason — you're sick, can't get into the home, the Service User is in hospital, or you're stuck and won't make it. Every missed visit must be reported and reallocated where possible.
lookFor: You'll be more than 30 minutes late to a visit
You can't get into the home — no answer, key not working, door locked
You're sick or can't make your shift
The Service User is in hospital, with family, or not at home
A previous visit overran and the next is now at risk
doNow: Call your supervisor immediately — never just not show up.
Can't get in? Try the agreed contact (family / keysafe). If still no entry, stay nearby and call the supervisor — don't leave the address until you've spoken to someone.
Sick? Call as early as possible, even out of hours — the earlier we know, the sooner we reallocate.
If a Service User seems unwell or vulnerable and you can't get in, call 999 / 112 if there is any concern.
whatRecord: Operations logs the missed visit in the CRM with a reason code
Diary note: time, address, and what you did to try to deliver

fieldGuide.name: A medication issue
fieldGuide.remember: We prompt — never administer. Every miss/refusal logged. Every error or near miss = incident report within 24h.
fieldGuide.whatItIs: Anything affecting the Service User's medication: a missed dose, tablets left over, a refusal, a new side effect, an apparent wrong tablet or wrong time. Medication is one of the highest-risk areas in home care — we are open about every event, even near misses.
lookFor: Tablets left in the blister pack when the week is over
A new symptom: drowsiness, confusion, rash, vomiting, swelling, reduced alertness
The Service User refuses their medication
A tablet that looks different from usual, or in a different place
Family pressuring you to administer (not prompt) — politely refuse
Any uncertainty about what to prompt or when
doNow: Stop. Do NOT administer — we only ever prompt.
For an adverse reaction, call the GP. If serious or the Service User is unresponsive, call 999.
Contact the Client Services Manager or Clinical Lead the same day.
Complete an incident report (CARE-20 + QA-13).
Log every prompt — including missed doses and refusals — in the CRM medication log.
whatRecord: Diary note; care plan updated if a pattern is emerging

fieldGuide.name: A fall — witnessed or unwitnessed
fieldGuide.remember: Every fall reported, even minor. Head injury + blood thinners = 999 even if symptom-free.
fieldGuide.whatItIs: Any time a Service User falls, slips, or is found on the floor — witnessed or not, even if they say they're fine. Falls in older adults are a leading cause of hospital admission and must always be reported and reviewed.
lookFor: Service User on the floor when you arrive
Bruising, swelling or pain that wasn't there before
They say they fell since your last visit
Increased unsteadiness or reluctance to walk
Furniture moved, rugs displaced, walking aid out of reach
doNow: Safety first: any sign of head injury, broken bone or unconsciousness — call 999.
If they seem unhurt, help them up using safe handling. Do NOT lift them yourself if it risks either of you.
Stay with them and monitor for delayed symptoms (especially head injury).
Contact the Client Services Manager the same day, even with no apparent injury.
Complete an incident report (CARE-32 + QA-13) before you leave shift.
On blood thinners and hit their head? Call the GP / 999 even with no symptoms.
whatRecord: Care plan & falls risk assessment reviewed and updated

fieldGuide.name: A safeguarding concern — adult at risk
fieldGuide.remember: Same day. Every time. No exceptions. Even if you’re not sure.
fieldGuide.whatItIs: Any sign that an adult Service User is being harmed, neglected, exploited or abused — by anyone. Physical, psychological, sexual, financial, neglect (including self-neglect) or discriminatory. Liberty Living has zero tolerance: every concern is taken seriously, recorded and acted on.
lookFor: Unexplained bruising, especially in unusual places
Flinching, withdrawal or anxiety around a specific person
Weight loss, dirty clothes, unwashed when usually clean
Money or possessions missing or unexplained
Pressure to change a will, sign documents, give cash gifts
Sudden change in behaviour, mood or willingness to talk
Deteriorating living conditions; spoiled food; unmanaged medication
doNow: Listen. Believe them. Stay calm.
Do NOT promise confidentiality — tell them you must share this with the Designated Safeguarding Officer.
Do NOT investigate, confront the alleged abuser, or warn anyone.
Ensure immediate safety — if anyone is in immediate danger, call 999.
Record exactly what was said in the Service User's own words; note date, time, who was present.
Contact the Designated Safeguarding Officer the same day — every time.
whoTell: Designated Safeguarding Officer (same day, every time)
whatRecord: Safeguarding concern form; verbatim record in the SU's words
Restricted-access safeguarding file (separate from the main record)

fieldGuide.name: A child protection concern
fieldGuide.remember: Same day to the DLP. Most HCAs are Mandated Persons — if the DLP doesn’t report, you must.
fieldGuide.whatItIs: Any concern about the welfare of a child in the Service User's home or family. The Children First Act 2015 places statutory obligations on Liberty Living — we MUST report concerns to Tusla. Most HCAs are Mandated Persons for the children they encounter through their work.
lookFor: Signs of neglect — child dirty, hungry, inadequately clothed
Bruising or injuries that don't match the explanation
Child appears afraid of, or cautious around, a specific adult
A disclosure from a child about anything that concerns you
Children left unsupervised when too young to be alone
Drug or alcohol misuse, or domestic violence, in front of children
doNow: Listen. Don't promise confidentiality.
Do NOT investigate or confront the alleged abuser.
Ensure the child's immediate safety — call 999 if in immediate danger.
Record exactly what you saw or heard — date, time, words used, who was present.
Contact the Designated Liaison Person (DLP) the same day.
The DLP reports to Tusla where the threshold is met. If you are a Mandated Person and the DLP does not report, you must report yourself.
whoTell: Designated Liaison Person (same day) — the DLP reports to Tusla
whatRecord: Child protection concern form; verbatim record

fieldGuide.name: Suspected financial abuse
fieldGuide.remember: Same day. Two-signatory rule on any SU money. Zero gifts policy.
fieldGuide.whatItIs: When something doesn't add up around the Service User's money, possessions or financial decisions. Financial abuse is among the most common — and hardest to spot — forms of abuse of older adults. We never handle SU money beyond what's strictly necessary, never accept gifts, and report any unexplained loss or pressure.
lookFor: Money or valuables missing from the home
SU can't pay for essentials but a relative is making large purchases
Pressure to change a will, give Power of Attorney, or sign documents
Unexplained account withdrawals
A visitor controlling access to the SU's money or cards
Family 'borrowing' money temporarily and not repaying
Receipts not matching what was bought
doNow: Do NOT challenge the suspected abuser or investigate.
Do NOT accept any money, gift, loan or item — politely refuse and explain it's policy.
If authorised to handle SU money, follow the two-signatory rule and keep every receipt.
Record exactly what you observed, using the SU's own words.
Contact the Client Services Manager AND Designated Safeguarding Officer the same day.
whatRecord: Cash log with receipts where SU money is handled by Liberty Living

fieldGuide.name: A complaint or concern
fieldGuide.remember: Same day to the Complaints Officer. Acknowledged in writing within 5 working days.
fieldGuide.whatItIs: Any expression of dissatisfaction, however small or informal — 'I'm not happy with…', 'Why didn't you…?', 'Last week the carer…'. These are complaints whether the person uses that word or not. Every complaint is logged, including anonymous and third-party ones.
lookFor: A verbal complaint during a visit
Family raising a concern by phone
A note left for you in the home
An email or text mentioning unhappiness
Body language: tense, dismissive, withholding
Repeated concerns that haven't been resolved
An anonymous note or letter
doNow: Listen. Acknowledge. Don't become defensive.
Say: 'Thank you for telling me. I'll make sure this is heard — I'll pass it to the Complaints Officer today.'
Don't promise an outcome or blame anyone — including yourself.
Write down exactly what they said and when, in their words.
Contact the Complaints Officer the same day.
If they're upset, offer the option to speak to the Client Services Manager directly.
whatRecord: Acknowledgement letter within 5 working days by the Complaints Officer

fieldGuide.name: An incident or near miss
fieldGuide.remember: Incident report within 24h — near misses included. Serious incident = same-day notification to CEO via Quality.
fieldGuide.whatItIs: Anything that went wrong, or could have. Beyond falls and medication (covered separately): someone got hurt, equipment failed, property or a vehicle was damaged, a fire, flood or power cut, an injury to staff, an aggressive interaction. Near misses count too — where nothing happened but easily could have.
lookFor: Service User or staff member hurt — even slightly
Equipment failure (hoist, bed, alarm)
Environmental hazard (gas leak, electrical fault, flood)
Aggressive or unsafe behaviour from anyone in the home
Property damage (yours or the SU's)
A 'lucky escape' — something could have gone badly wrong
A vehicle incident on the way to a visit
doNow: Make the situation safe first. Get medical help if anyone is hurt.
Contact your supervisor as soon as it's safe to do so.
Complete an incident report within 24 hours — especially for near misses.
If serious (severe injury, suspected crime, fire, hazard) call 999 / utilities and tell the supervisor immediately.
whatRecord: Incident report within 24 hours; diary note

fieldGuide.name: A change in the Service User's condition
fieldGuide.remember: Same day for any change you'd want to know about if it were your parent.
fieldGuide.whatItIs: You know the Service User best — you see them on a regular schedule. If they seem 'off', that matters. Subtle changes in older adults often signal serious underlying problems. Always trust what you're seeing.
lookFor: More confused than usual, or less alert
New unsteadiness or balance issues
Skin changes — pale, flushed, sweaty, mottled, new bruising
Reduced appetite or fluid intake
New or worsening pain
New cough, shortness of breath, breathing differently
Changes in continence
Withdrawn, low mood, expressing hopelessness
New skin breakdown or pressure damage
doNow: Ask how they're feeling — listen to what they say and don't say.
Check vitals if trained and equipped (temperature, pulse).
Compare to their usual baseline. Trust your instinct.
Anything acute — severe pain, change in consciousness, breathing problems — call 999.
Otherwise contact the GP or PHN the same day and inform the Client Services Manager.
Record everything in the diary so the next visitor sees the picture.
whatRecord: Care plan review prompt to the Clinical Lead
Incident report if a deterioration was previously missed

fieldGuide.name: Infection control concern
fieldGuide.remember: Hand hygiene 20+ seconds. Symptomatic staff = stay home. Outbreaks = immediate escalation.
fieldGuide.whatItIs: Anything that could spread infection between Service Users, into the home, or to you — especially in respiratory-virus season. Hand hygiene is the single most effective control, and the most commonly missed.
lookFor: SU symptomatic — cough, fever, vomiting, diarrhoea
A family member or visitor symptomatic
PPE running out or compromised
No hand-hygiene facilities (no soap, clean water or sanitiser)
Confirmed infection in the home (COVID, flu, MRSA, C. diff, scabies)
A wound that smells, looks infected, or hasn't been managed
You feeling unwell — and considering coming to work
doNow: Perform hand hygiene before and after every visit — 20+ seconds with soap, or alcohol sanitiser.
Use the right PPE — don't economise.
Have symptoms? DO NOT come to work — call in and let Operations reallocate.
If the SU has symptoms of infection, contact the GP / PHN the same day and update the care plan with isolation precautions.
Report PPE shortages to your supervisor immediately.
whatRecord: Incident report for outbreaks or breaches

fieldGuide.name: Lone worker concern — your own safety
fieldGuide.remember: Your safety > the visit. Every time.
fieldGuide.whatItIs: Liberty Living staff work alone in private homes. Most of the time it's safe and routine — sometimes it isn't. Aggressive visitors, intoxicated household members, unsafe environments, threats. Your safety is the priority.
lookFor: Aggressive behaviour from the SU, family or a visitor
Intoxicated household members
Threatening dogs not restrained
Unsafe environment — broken stairs, no lighting, fire hazards
Inappropriate behaviour towards you
A feeling that you might be in danger
A home or area flagged previously
doNow: Trust your instinct — your safety comes first.
Feel unsafe at the door? Do NOT enter. Step away and call your supervisor.
Unsafe inside? Leave — use a natural reason ('I need to get something from the car').
In immediate danger, call 999.
Contact your supervisor once you're safe. Don't continue your shift if you've been shaken.
Complete an incident report — every lone-worker incident informs future planning.
whatRecord: Care plan may need a second-worker provision added

fieldGuide.name: A concern about a colleague's conduct
fieldGuide.remember: You are protected when you raise a concern in good faith (QA-37).
fieldGuide.whatItIs: If you see another staff member doing something that puts a Service User or anyone else at risk, or breaching professional boundaries, you have a duty to raise it. The Protected Disclosure policy means you can do this safely — confidentially and without fear of penalty.
lookFor: A colleague administering (not prompting) medication
Accepting money, gifts or favours from a Service User
Inappropriate physical contact
Disrespectful or dismissive treatment of a Service User
Falsifying records (visit timings, medication logs)
Smelling of alcohol on duty
Sharing SU information on personal social media
Building inappropriate personal relationships with SUs or families
doNow: Don't confront the colleague directly — that risks the situation and your evidence.
Record exactly what you saw — date, time, location, words.
Speak to your Supervisor or Client Services Manager.
Worried about retaliation? Raise a Protected Disclosure (QA-37) — confidentially.
If safeguarding is involved, contact the Designated Safeguarding Officer directly.

fieldGuide.name: A documentation gap
fieldGuide.remember: Don't write retrospective records for missed days — note the gap honestly.
fieldGuide.whatItIs: If you arrive and find previous records missing, incomplete, or contradicting what you're seeing, that's a data-integrity issue. Records are how we know what care has been delivered — gaps and errors must be flagged so the picture stays accurate.
lookFor: Medication log missing entries for previous days
Care plan doesn't reflect the SU's current needs
Risk assessment more than 12 months old
A previous visit not signed off
Information in the home that isn't in the CRM
Inconsistency between the CRM and what the SU says
doNow: Document what you observe in the diary AND the CRM.
Don't retrospectively complete someone else's record — note the gap and report it.
Tell your supervisor — they'll log the gap and arrange follow-up.
If it's a clinical risk (e.g. an out-of-date risk assessment), flag to the Clinical Lead.

flRoles.label: HCA — in the home
flRoles.mission: You are the eyes and ears of the service. The office only knows what you tell us. Spot it, do the right thing in the moment, and tell the right person the same day. When in doubt — ASK.
owns: Spotting changes, risks and concerns in the home
Acting safely in the moment — 999 first if anyone is in danger
Reporting the same day — supervisor, CSM, DSO or Complaints Officer
Recording what you saw, in the Service User's own words, in the diary and CRM

flRoles.label: Care Coordinators — office & phone
flRoles.mission: You run the response from the office. You take the call, log it correctly, reallocate the work, and drive every policy and SOP to closure — so the front line is supported and nothing slips.
owns: Taking calls and logging every event in the CRM with the right reason code
Reallocating visits and keeping the roster safe and covered
Routing each concern to the right owner the same day — CSM, DSO, DLP, Complaints Officer
Issuing acknowledgements, tracking timeframes and progressing every report to close
Keeping records, restricted files and document control clean and in date

flRoles.label: Client Service Managers
flRoles.mission: You own the outcome. You triage what comes in, make the clinical and risk decisions with the Clinical Lead, keep families informed, and sign off — escalating to the CEO, HSE or authorities where the policy requires.
owns: Triaging incidents, complaints and concerns and deciding the response
Owning care-plan, risk-assessment and MAR reviews with the Clinical Lead
Signing off incidents and complaints and capturing learning
Escalating to CEO / HSE / Tusla / Gardaí where the policy threshold is met

coord: Take the call; log the late/missed visit in the CRM with the correct reason code (>30 late · >60 or non-delivery = missed).
Reallocate to another carer where possible, or agree a safe alternative with the Service User / family.
No entry or welfare doubt? Direct the carer, arrange a welfare check, escalate to on-call out of hours.
Update the roster; flag any pattern or unfilled visit to the Client Services Manager.
csm: Assure the Service User's safety is covered; authorise any welfare check or family contact.
Review repeat missed visits for a service-continuity or safeguarding risk (GOV-25).
Own the family conversation where trust is affected; feed into contract / KPI review.

coord: Open the incident report (QA-13) and flag the CRM medication log.
Notify the Client Services Manager and Clinical Lead the same day; record GP contact.
Track that the care plan / MAR is updated and the incident is progressed to close.
csm: Triage the medication event same day; decide GP / 999 follow-up with the Clinical Lead.
Own the review — root cause, retraining, care-plan / MAR update.
Sign off the incident; notify family and, where reportable, HSE / pharmacy.

coord: Log the fall and incident report (QA-13); update the falls log.
Notify the Client Services Manager same day; record GP / 999 contact.
Ensure the falls risk-assessment review is scheduled and captured.
csm: Review every fall; decide on GP / OT / physio referral and care-plan changes.
Own the falls risk-assessment update and any environmental actions.
Notify family; escalate serious falls per QA-13.

coord: Route to the Designated Safeguarding Officer the SAME DAY — never sit on it.
Keep safeguarding detail OUT of the open CRM; file to the restricted safeguarding record only.
Support the DSO with roster, history and contacts; log the referral timeline.
csm: With the DSO, assess risk and immediate safety; decide on 999 / HSE Safeguarding referral.
Coordinate the protection plan and the staff involved; hold the restricted file.
Manage family / agency communication; feed learning back to the team.

coord: Get the concern to the Designated Liaison Person the SAME DAY.
File to the restricted child-protection record only — not the open CRM.
Support the DLP; log the referral and any Tusla report timeline.
csm: With the DLP, apply the Children First threshold and ensure Tusla reporting where met.
Coordinate any joint response and support the staff member.
Maintain the restricted file and confidentiality.

coord: Route to the Client Services Manager AND DSO same day; file to the restricted record.
Check the cash log / two-signatory records and receipts where Liberty handles SU money.
Log the referral timeline; do not discuss with the household.
csm: Assess and, with the DSO, decide on HSE Safeguarding / Garda referral.
Audit any Liberty handling of SU money against CARE-17; tighten controls.
Manage the protection plan and communication.

coord: Log the complaint the same day (QA-03) — verbal, anonymous and third-party included.
Issue the written acknowledgement within 5 working days.
Assign to the Complaints Officer / CSM, start the response clock and track to resolution.
csm: Own the investigation and response; keep the complainant updated.
Decide the outcome and remedial actions; capture the learning.
Escalate unresolved or serious complaints per QA-03.2.

coord: Log the incident report within 24h (near misses included); open the CRM record.
Notify the CSM same day; for serious incidents, same-day notification to the CEO via Quality.
Track corrective actions and any RCA to close.
csm: Triage severity; decide on 999 / authority notification and whether an RCA is needed.
Own corrective actions and any care-plan review.
Sign off; notify family / HSE where reportable.

coord: Log the reported change; ensure GP / PHN contact is recorded and the CSM informed same day.
Flag a care-plan review to the Clinical Lead.
Track any resulting visit or roster changes.
csm: Review the change; decide GP / PHN / 999 and care-plan updates with the Clinical Lead.
Own the reassessment and any increased support.
Keep the family informed.

coord: Log PPE shortages and reallocate symptomatic staff off shift.
Record SU illness and GP / PHN contact; update the care plan with isolation precautions.
Escalate any suspected outbreak to the Clinical Lead immediately.
csm: Own the outbreak response and isolation plan with the Clinical Lead.
Ensure PPE supply and staff cohorting.
Notify HSE / PHN where required; review IPC compliance.

coord: Take the call, confirm the worker is safe, and reallocate or abandon the visit as needed.
Log the incident report (HS-16) and update the address risk flag.
Escalate to on-call out of hours; inform the CSM.
csm: Review the lone-worker risk; decide on second-worker provision or visit suspension.
Own the risk-assessment update and any Garda involvement.
Support the affected staff member.

coord: Route to the Supervisor / CSM confidentially — do not broadcast.
Where a Protected Disclosure (QA-37) is made, use the confidential CEO channel.
File to the confidential HR record only; log the concern.
csm: Assess and, where safeguarding, involve the DSO; decide on precautionary action.
Own the HR / disciplinary process (HR-38) with confidentiality and worker protection.
Manage any regulator or insurer notification.

coord: Log the records-audit issue; never create retrospective entries.
Assign follow-up to correct the record and inform the CSM.
Clinical gap (e.g. out-of-date risk assessment)? Flag to the Clinical Lead.
csm: Own correction of the record and the underlying cause.
Ensure risk assessments and care plans are back in date.
Feed repeated gaps into audit and supervision.
